metadata.title: Claude, Judged
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent; callout ink #161511 on cream; code stays Onda dark glass
metadata.note: Audience-namespaced adaptation. Facts and visual argument preserved from the canonical reel. B08 states when to use and not use AI; B09 is the audience outro.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Gate P is a deterministic preflight that blocks spending but never proves the finished work is good. Determinable questions follow from inspectable evidence; judgeable ones depend on purpose and audience. Automate the determinable checks early and cheaply, before rendering and review consume time. Publication authority stays with a named human who weighs values, audience, and consequences.

artifactLines: Gate P is a deterministic preflight that blocks spending but never proves the finished work is good.
Determinable questions follow from inspectable evidence; judgeable ones depend on purpose and audience.
Automate the determinable checks early and cheaply, before rendering and review consume time.
Publication authority stays with a named human who weighs values, audience, and consequences.

beats.narration_text: The determinable-versus-judgeable split is the cleanest rule I've heard for what to automate — file exists and schema passes on one side, useful and fair and publishable on the other. I want to apply it to my own review pipeline for blog posts. Can you (1) sort a list of checks I'll give you into determinable and judgeable; (2) design one cheap early gate like Gate P that blocks spending before rendering starts; (3) specify exactly what a human reviewer must still sign off on?

props.command: The determinable-versus-judgeable split is the cleanest rule I've heard for what to automate — file exists and schema passes on one side, useful and fair and publishable on the other. I want to apply it to my own review pipeline for blog posts. Can you (1) sort a list of checks I'll give you into determinable and judgeable; (2) design one cheap early gate like Gate P that blocks spending before rendering starts; (3) specify exactly what a human reviewer must still sign off on?

beats.narration_text: Gate P is a deterministic preflight check. It looks for VERDICT: PASS before audio generation starts. Use it to block avoidable spending. Do not treat it as evidence that the finished video is good; it never watched the cut.

props.title: GATE P — generate_audio.py
props.code:
ped = Path(slug_dir) / 'PEDAGOGY.md'
if not re.search(
    r'^\s*VERDICT:\s*PASS\b', ped.read_text()
):
    print('[err] GATE P: no VERDICT: PASS')
    sys.exit(3)
# cheap. deterministic. correct.

beats.narration_text: Separate determinable questions from judgeable ones. Determinable means the answer follows from inspectable evidence: file exists, timing matches, schema passes. Judgeable means the answer depends on purpose and audience: useful, clear, responsible, publishable. Automate the first category. Assign the second to a named person.

props.heading: Determinable vs Judgeable
props.body: Determinable: file exists, timing matches, schema passes. Judgeable: useful, clear, responsible, publishable. Automate the first. Assign the second to a named person.

beats.narration_text: Use cheap gates early. They catch structural defects before audio, rendering, and review consume time. Do not stack gates around criteria nobody can state. A vague gate creates ceremony, not control.

props.heading: Use cheap gates early
props.body: They catch structural defects before audio, rendering, and review consume time. Do not stack gates around criteria nobody can state — a vague gate creates ceremony, not control.

beats.narration_text: Delegate checks with machine-readable evidence: beat count, duration, resolution, paths, and QC artifacts. Require the agent to report what it inspected. Do not delegate claims it cannot verify from those artifacts.

props.heading: Delegate with machine-readable evidence
props.body: Beat count, duration, resolution, paths, QC artifacts. Require the agent to report what it inspected. Do not delegate claims it cannot verify.

beats.narration_text: Keep publication authority with a human reviewer. The reviewer decides whether the pacing serves the idea, whether the framing is fair, and whether the work earns attention. The agent supplies evidence and flags. It does not sign off.

props.heading: Publication authority stays human
props.body: The reviewer decides whether pacing serves the idea, whether framing is fair, whether the work earns attention. The agent supplies evidence and flags. It does not sign off.

beats.narration_text: The operating model is consistent: route by cost, scope risk, encode stable judgment, pin exact requirements, expect variation, steer with context, and name the approver. Use automation to reduce repeated checking. Do not use it to erase accountability.

props.heading: The operating model
props.body: Route by cost, scope risk, encode stable judgment, pin exact requirements, expect variation, steer with context, name the approver. Reduce checking. Do not erase accountability.

beats.narration_text: Decision rule: if success can be stated as an observable test, automate the check. If success depends on values, audience, or consequences, use AI for evidence gathering and keep the decision human. Record who checked what.

artifactLines: Agents check the determinable: schema, timing, paths, resolution.
Humans check the judgeable: interesting, worth watching, publish.
Gates are cheap taste-checks before expensive ones.
The determinism boundary is the human-judgment boundary.

beats.narration_text: Use AI when the criterion is explicit, the evidence is available, and a wrong result can be reviewed or reversed. Do not use AI as the final authority for audience impact, fairness, or publication. Have it produce an audit table, then require a named human decision.

props.artifactTitle: When to use AI — and when not to
artifactLines: USE — explicit criteria · available evidence · reversible action
DO NOT USE AS FINAL AUTHORITY — impact · fairness · publication

cli_exercise.ask: claude "Audit this workflow. Return a table with: decision, observable evidence, safe to automate, human approver, and failure consequence. Do not make the final approval decision."
cli_exercise.output_description: A decision-rights table separating automated checks from accountable human approvals.
cli_exercise.change: Add a reversibility column and flag every irreversible or public-facing action.
cli_exercise.output2_description: The same table with high-risk handoffs highlighted for mandatory human review.
cli_exercise.next_step: Run it on your own publishing or service-delivery workflow.

beats.narration_text: Apply the method with Humanitarians AI. More at humanitarians.ai.
